**OpenClaw** is an open-source, self-hosted AI assistant framework that acts as a **gateway** between user-facing messaging apps and one or more AI “agent” backends. At its core, OpenClaw runs a long-lived **Gateway** process on your machine that integrates with many communication channels (WhatsApp, Telegram, Slack, Discord, **Microsoft Teams** via a plugin, a local web UI, etc.) and routes incoming messages or events to an **AI agent runtime** powered by large language models. The **agent** (e.g. a coding assistant based on Anthropic’s Claude or OpenAI’s GPT-4) carries out the “thinking” – it receives each input along with context and **prompts** assembled by OpenClaw, uses various **tools/skills** to perform tasks (like browsing the web, running code, or sending messages), and produces a response. Meanwhile, the Gateway manages all I/O, event scheduling, and state persistence on the local machine. In summary, **OpenClaw’s architecture can be seen as a hub-and-spoke model with the Node.js *Gateway* at the center (hub) coordinating multiple *agents* and *channels* (spokes)**. Below, we break down the key components, how data and prompts are handled, and how the system executes workflows – with an eye towards how you can recreate its core functionality in a local Electron-based app. [\[insiderllm.com\]](https://www.insiderllm.com/guides/how-openclaw-works/), [\[insiderllm.com\]](https://www.insiderllm.com/guides/how-openclaw-works/) [\[deepwiki.com\]](https://deepwiki.com/openclaw/openclaw), [\[deepwiki.com\]](https://deepwiki.com/openclaw/openclaw)

<!-- Copilot-Researcher-Visualization -->

![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

### Architecture & Key Components

At a high level, OpenClaw is composed of a **Gateway** service (written in Node.js/TypeScript) and one or more **AI agents** (LLM-powered reasoning engines, running *within* the same process via an “embedded” runtime library). Surrounding these core pieces are a collection of subsystems: connectors for chat apps (channels), a tool/skill system for executing actions, persistent storage for memory and session data, and user interface components. The table below summarizes some of the **key files/modules** in the OpenClaw repository and their roles in the system:

<!-- Copilot-Researcher-Visualization -->

![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

| **Module / File**                                               | **Role in System**                   | **Description & Relationships**                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      |
| --------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------ | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `openclaw.mjs` (CLI entry point)                                | **Launcher & CLI**                   | Main entry script that starts the Gateway server and provides CLI commands. Running `openclaw.mjs` (via `openclaw` CLI) launches the **Gateway** – a long-lived Node.js process that acts as the control plane. It loads the configuration, initializes channels, and listens on a local port (default **18789**) for the Control UI and API [\[deepwiki.com\]](https://deepwiki.com/openclaw/openclaw), [\[deepwiki.com\]](https://deepwiki.com/openclaw/openclaw).                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 |
| **Gateway Core** (`src/gateway/…`)                              | **Hub / Control Plane**              | Manages all high-level functionality: starting the WebSocket & HTTP server, handling **multiple chat connections**, routing incoming events to the appropriate agent, and managing global state (sessions, config, logging). The Gateway is the **“single source of truth”** for session info, channel status, and routing rules [\[docs.openclaw.ai\]](https://docs.openclaw.ai/). It ensures each incoming event (message, timer, webhook, etc.) is validated (e.g. checking *DM pairing* codes and allow-lists for security [\[github.com\]](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw)) and then placed into the agent’s task queue for processing [\[insiderllm.com\]](https://www.insiderllm.com/guides/how-openclaw-works/), [\[insiderllm.com\]](https://www.insiderllm.com/guides/how-openclaw-works/).                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          |
| **Agent Runtime** (`src/agent/…` incl. `runEmbeddedPiAgent.ts`) | **LLM + Tools Orchestrator**         | Handles the **AI logic** of each agent. OpenClaw uses a library called **Pi Agent Core** (`@mariozechner/pi-agent-core`) as the backbone for running LLM inference and tool invocation in code. The `runEmbeddedPiAgent` function is the primary loop that processes one “turn”: it loads the relevant agent’s profile and **workspace** files (see below), builds the **system prompt** with all necessary context, calls the LLM via its API (Anthropic Claude is recommended, but OpenAI GPT-4 and others are supported [\[github.com\]](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw)), and **parses the model’s output to detect tool-use commands** [\[deepwiki.com\]](https://deepwiki.com/openclaw/openclaw/5-agent-system), [\[deepwiki.com\]](https://deepwiki.com/openclaw/openclaw/5-agent-system). If the model’s response includes an action (e.g. a special token or JSON indicating a tool invocation), the Agent runtime calls the corresponding tool function, feeds the result back into the model if needed, and iterates until the task is complete. This is how complex “**workflow**” executions are choreographed – the LLM plans and requests tool actions, and the runtime executes them step by step. Finally, the agent’s answer (or tool results) are returned to the user via the Gateway.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     |
| **Channels** (`src/channels/…` and `extensions/…`)              | **Messaging Integrations**           | Connectors that link chat platforms to the Gateway. OpenClaw has built-in modules for major apps – e.g. **WhatsApp** via the Baileys library, **Telegram** via grammY, **Slack** via @slack/bolt, **Discord**, **Apple iMessage** (via BlueBubbles), **Google Chat**, etc [\[deepwiki.com\]](https://deepwiki.com/openclaw/openclaw). **Microsoft Teams** is supported through a plugin that utilizes the Azure Bot/Graph API (installed via `openclaw plugins install @openclaw/msteams`). Each channel module handles service-specific APIs or WebSocket connections: incoming messages are converted into a standard OpenClaw event format and passed to the Gateway’s router, and agent replies are relayed back out as messages on the same channel [\[deepwiki.com\]](https://deepwiki.com/openclaw/openclaw), [\[deepwiki.com\]](https://deepwiki.com/openclaw/openclaw). This design lets a single AI agent **seamlessly communicate across multiple platforms** at once.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    |
| **Session Store** (`src/session/` and `~/.openclaw/sessions/`)  | **Conversation State Persistence**   | Tracks active **sessions** and saves conversation history. Each chat **Session** is identified by a key in the form `<agentId>:<channel>:<userID or threadID>` (for example, `main:whatsapp:+1234555…` for a WhatsApp DM) [\[deepwiki.com\]](https://deepwiki.com/openclaw/openclaw), [\[deepwiki.com\]](https://deepwiki.com/openclaw/openclaw). OpenClaw maintains a **session log** for each session: as messages and agent replies are exchanged, they are appended to a JSONL file on disk (under `~/.openclaw/sessions/`) [\[deepwiki.com\]](https://deepwiki.com/openclaw/openclaw). This ensures that if the system restarts, the conversation history can be reloaded, and it also allows the agent to “remember” prior interactions. The Session subsystem also handles **history compaction** – when the context becomes too large, older messages are summarized or moved into long-term memory (see *Memory* below) to stay within LLM token limits [\[docs.openclaw.ai\]](https://docs.openclaw.ai/concepts/memory), [\[docs.openclaw.ai\]](https://docs.openclaw.ai/concepts/memory).                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 |
| **Workspace & Memory** (e.g. `~/.openclaw/workspace/`)          | **Agent’s Files & Long-Term Memory** | Each agent has a dedicated *workspace* directory (by default under `~/.openclaw/workspace/`) where it stores knowledge about the user and past interactions [\[docs.openclaw.ai\]](https://docs.openclaw.ai/concepts/memory). This includes files like:<ul><li>`AGENTS.md` – descriptions of agents/personas (for multi-agent setups).</li><li>`IDENTITY.md` and `USER.md` – info about the user and any defined persona or “soul” of the assistant.</li><li>`TOOLS.md` – descriptions of available tools.</li><li>`HEARTBEAT.md` – instructions for periodic background tasks (heartbeats).</li><li>A `memory/` folder with daily logs (`YYYY-MM-DD.md`) and an optional `MEMORY.md` for long-term facts [\[docs.openclaw.ai\]](https://docs.openclaw.ai/concepts/memory).</li></ul>These Markdown files are maintained by the agent itself – for example, the agent might append notes to the daily log or update MEMORY.md when told to “remember” something. **On each new query or event, OpenClaw reads these files and injects their content into the LLM’s context (prompt)** so the agent has persistent memory of past dialogues and user preferences [\[docs.openclaw.ai\]](https://docs.openclaw.ai/concepts/memory), [\[insiderllm.com\]](https://www.insiderllm.com/guides/how-openclaw-works/). (Notably, OpenClaw can also build a vector index of memory files using SQLite or an external service to allow semantic search through past notes [\[docs.openclaw.ai\]](https://docs.openclaw.ai/concepts/memory), [\[docs.openclaw.ai\]](https://docs.openclaw.ai/concepts/memory).) |
| **Tools & Execution** (`src/tools/` and policies in config)     | **Extended Capabilities**            | **Tools** are functions the agent can use to act on your system or fetch information. OpenClaw comes with numerous built-in tools (for example: `exec` to run shell commands, `browser` to control a headless browser, `read`/`write` to access files, `email` to send emails, `webhook` to call external APIs, `cron` to schedule tasks, etc.) [\[deepwiki.com\]](https://deepwiki.com/openclaw/openclaw). The available tools (and their usage syntax) are enumerated to the AI in the system prompt, allowing the model to invoke them by name. For instance, if the model’s response includes a token or JSON indicating a `browser.search` action, the Gateway will execute that tool and feed the result back to the model for further processing [\[deepwiki.com\]](https://deepwiki.com/openclaw/openclaw), [\[insiderllm.com\]](https://www.insiderllm.com/guides/how-openclaw-works/). Tool use is tightly controlled via **policies** – the configuration (`openclaw.json`) can specify allow/deny lists globally or per agent to prevent misuse of destructive commands [\[deepwiki.com\]](https://deepwiki.com/openclaw/openclaw). Sandboxing is also available: in non-“main” sessions or when configured, OpenClaw can execute tools within a Docker container for isolation [\[deepwiki.com\]](https://deepwiki.com/openclaw/openclaw).                                                                                                                                                                                                                                              |
| **Skills** (`skills/` & `src/skills/`)                          | **Custom Scripts & Workflows**       | **Skills** in OpenClaw are pluggable modules (often installed from a marketplace called “ClawHub”) that bundle new tools or complex workflows. A skill typically consists of a directory with a manifest (`SKILL.md` describing what the skill does and how to use it) plus any code or scripts needed. OpenClaw can load “**managed skills**” (installed system-wide) or **workspace skills** (for a specific agent) from the `skills/` folder [\[deepwiki.com\]](https://deepwiki.com/openclaw/openclaw). For example, a skill might provide a new tool for controlling smart home devices, or define a multi-step workflow for a task like job application. Skills are referenced in the system prompt’s **Skills** section as available abilities, and the agent can explicitly load and run them (often by using the `read` tool to read the skill’s instructions or script) [\[docs.openclaw.ai\]](https://docs.openclaw.ai/concepts/system-prompt), [\[docs.openclaw.ai\]](https://docs.openclaw.ai/concepts/system-prompt). This design allows you to extend the assistant’s functionality or share workflows without modifying the core code – **in your own Electron app, you could adopt a similar pattern (e.g. define JSON/YAML or Markdown “workflow” files that your agent can load and execute as new capabilities).**                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                               |

**Data Persistence & Memory:** All key state in OpenClaw is stored locally on your machine in a structured way. By default, the “**state directory**” is `~/.openclaw/` (configurable via environment variables). Important data stored here includes: [\[deepwiki.com\]](https://deepwiki.com/openclaw/openclaw), [\[deepwiki.com\]](https://deepwiki.com/openclaw/openclaw)

*   **Main configuration** (`openclaw.json`), which contains settings for channels, agents, model providers, and security policies. This is a JSON5 file that gets created by the onboarding wizard and can be edited to tweak behavior. Environment variables or CLI flags can override config values at runtime. [\[deepwiki.com\]](https://deepwiki.com/openclaw/openclaw)
*   **Session logs** (`sessions/` folder with one `<session>.jsonl` per conversation) – these track the running conversation history for each session in chronological order. Each turn (user message, agent reply, or automated event) is appended as a JSON line. On startup, the Gateway loads `sessions.json` (an index of session metadata) to restore recent context. [\[deepwiki.com\]](https://deepwiki.com/openclaw/openclaw)
*   **Agent workspace files** (under `workspace/`) – as described above, each agent’s folder stores persistent memory and instructions in Markdown files (e.g. daily logs under `workspace/memory/` and long-term `MEMORY.md`). The agent’s code uses these files as the **ground truth memory**. For example, when an agent is close to forgetting context due to token limits, it will be prompted (via an automated *memory flush* hook) to summarize important info into the memory files before truncating the conversation. This way, the next time the agent runs, it can reload those files and recall past knowledge. [\[docs.openclaw.ai\]](https://docs.openclaw.ai/concepts/memory) [\[docs.openclaw.ai\]](https://docs.openclaw.ai/concepts/memory), [\[docs.openclaw.ai\]](https://docs.openclaw.ai/concepts/memory)
*   **Credentials and Keys** – API keys or OAuth tokens for model providers and integration services are stored in dedicated files (`auth-profiles.json` for LLM API keys/tokens, and a `credentials/` subfolder for channel-specific tokens like Slack bot tokens or WhatsApp login info). This keeps secrets separate from the main config. [\[deepwiki.com\]](https://deepwiki.com/openclaw/openclaw), [\[deepwiki.com\]](https://deepwiki.com/openclaw/openclaw)
*   **Logging and usage data** – OpenClaw logs events and errors to a `logs/` directory (rotating daily logs in JSONL format). It also tracks token usage per session to help manage model context windows and costs. [\[deepwiki.com\]](https://deepwiki.com/openclaw/openclaw)

OpenClaw’s design emphasizes *local-first data storage* for privacy. All conversation context and memory reside on your machine unless you configure an external persistence (for example, in a Cloudflare Workers deployment, it can use Cloudflare R2 storage to persist data across restarts). In a single-machine setup, you could follow this model by using local files or a lightweight database (like SQLite) to save conversation history and any long-term memory that your agent might need. [\[aicybr.com\]](https://aicybr.com/blog/openclaw-guide)

### Prompt Design and Use of LLMs

OpenClaw employs a sophisticated **prompting system** to control the AI agent’s behavior and enable tool usage. Rather than relying on a fixed prompt baked into the model, OpenClaw dynamically **assembles a System Prompt** on each agent turn. This system prompt is “OpenClaw-owned,” meaning the framework defines it completely (overriding any default prompt the base model might have). The prompt is designed to be compact yet rich in critical information, divided into sections that are injected into the model’s context each time: [\[docs.openclaw.ai\]](https://docs.openclaw.ai/concepts/system-prompt)

*   **Tooling** – A list of the tools available to the agent (by name), each with a one-line description of its function. This informs the model of actions it can perform (such as `<code>exec</code>` for running shell commands or `<code>browser.search</code>` for web browsing). The model is instructed on *how* to invoke tools by producing specific tokens or formatted outputs that the OpenClaw runtime will recognize and execute. [\[docs.openclaw.ai\]](https://docs.openclaw.ai/concepts/system-prompt)
*   **Safety** – Guidelines to discourage disallowed behavior (e.g. reminders not to pursue harmful tasks or bypass oversight). These are advisory guardrails for the LLM, supplementing any safety measures the model itself has. [\[docs.openclaw.ai\]](https://docs.openclaw.ai/concepts/system-prompt)
*   **Skills** – If any skill plugins are installed and relevant, the prompt includes an **`<available_skills>` list**. Instead of inlining each skill’s full instructions, OpenClaw gives the model a list of skill names/descriptions and a file path for each skill’s documentation. The model is told it can use the `read` tool to open a skill’s **SKILL.md** file when needed. This indirection keeps the prompt short while still allowing the model to tap into extensive instructions on demand. [\[docs.openclaw.ai\]](https://docs.openclaw.ai/concepts/system-prompt), [\[docs.openclaw.ai\]](https://docs.openclaw.ai/concepts/system-prompt)
*   **Self-Update Instructions** – Hints that the assistant can update or improve itself by writing to its config or even updating its own code (for example, how to call `config.apply` or run an `update` command). This is part of OpenClaw’s vision of an AI that can modify its behavior or add skills autonomously (though in practice such actions might be restricted by user approvals). [\[docs.openclaw.ai\]](https://docs.openclaw.ai/concepts/system-prompt)
*   **Workspace & Docs** – The prompt tells the agent what its current working directory is and where it can find documentation. It provides a path or link to the local OpenClaw **docs** and instructs the agent to consult those docs (or use a `openclaw status` command) if it needs help understanding the platform’s operation. This is basically a built-in form of ***“self-referential” documentation access*** for the AI, so it knows how to use the OpenClaw APIs and behaviors correctly by reading the manual itself when necessary. [\[docs.openclaw.ai\]](https://docs.openclaw.ai/concepts/system-prompt), [\[docs.openclaw.ai\]](https://docs.openclaw.ai/concepts/system-prompt)
*   **Bootstrap Context** – OpenClaw will automatically inject the contents of certain key files from the agent’s workspace at the end of the system prompt (labeled as “Project Context”). These include the agent’s identity/profile (`AGENTS.md` and `SOUL.md` if present), the user profile (`IDENTITY.md` / `USER.md`), tool definitions (`TOOLS.md`), the heartbeat schedule (`HEARTBEAT.md`), and any long-term memory files (`MEMORY.md` or the latest daily log). By inserting these into the prompt, the agent is primed with recent context and facts *before* seeing the user’s query or task. (Large files may be truncated to stay within token limits; only the main private chat session gets full long-term memory by default, whereas group chats or sub-agents use minimal context for efficiency.) [\[docs.openclaw.ai\]](https://docs.openclaw.ai/concepts/system-prompt), [\[docs.openclaw.ai\]](https://docs.openclaw.ai/concepts/system-prompt)
*   **Runtime and Context Tags** – Additional sections include the current date/time and timezone, a special marker for “reply tags” (used to format responses for certain channels), notes about whether the agent is running in **sandboxed mode**, and an indicator of the agent’s current “thinking depth” or verbosity setting. These help the model be aware of its operating context. [\[docs.openclaw.ai\]](https://docs.openclaw.ai/concepts/system-prompt), [\[docs.openclaw.ai\]](https://docs.openclaw.ai/concepts/system-prompt)

All these pieces are concatenated into one large system-level prompt string. The **user’s message or the event’s instruction** is then appended after this system prompt, and the whole prompt is sent to the LLM. **In effect, every single agent response is generated in the context of a rich, structured prompt that OpenClaw controls**, ensuring the AI knows about its tools, memory, and instructions for autonomy each time it runs. (For a deeper dive into the exact prompt template, see the *System Prompt* documentation.) [\[docs.openclaw.ai\]](https://docs.openclaw.ai/concepts/system-prompt), [\[docs.openclaw.ai\]](https://docs.openclaw.ai/concepts/system-prompt)

### How OpenClaw Executes Tasks (Orchestration & Workflows)

At runtime, OpenClaw follows an **event-driven orchestration loop** that makes the agent *appear* to act autonomously. The process can be described as a continuous cycle of receiving events, queuing tasks, invoking the LLM, and executing tools, as described in the project’s architecture guides: [\[insiderllm.com\]](https://www.insiderllm.com/guides/how-openclaw-works/), [\[insiderllm.com\]](https://www.insiderllm.com/guides/how-openclaw-works/)

1.  **Event Intake:** The Gateway collects inputs from **multiple sources** – not only user chat messages, but also scheduled **heartbeats** (timers), **cron jobs**, **webhook events** (e.g. incoming emails or calendar alerts), and internal **hooks** (like startup or shutdown events). Each of these is normalized into a common format (essentially a message or command with an associated session context). For example, a heartbeat event might carry a prompt like “Check my email and report important updates.” Every event is tagged with the target *agent* (by default there is one agent named “main,” but you can have multiple agents for different purposes) and the source channel or trigger type. [\[insiderllm.com\]](https://www.insiderllm.com/guides/how-openclaw-works/), [\[insiderllm.com\]](https://www.insiderllm.com/guides/how-openclaw-works/)
2.  **Queue & Session Routing:** Events are placed into a queue managed by the Gateway. OpenClaw ensures that for a given session (e.g. your personal WhatsApp chat with the assistant, or a particular scheduled task for the main agent), events are processed sequentially, so the agent handles one task at a time in each session. The session logic uses the event’s metadata to find or create the right session file (using the key format described earlier) and loads any recent history or context needed. This mechanism is what enables **multi-agent setups**: you can have separate session queues (and even different model configs or tools) for different agents, all managed by the single Gateway process. Agents can also send messages to each other by enqueuing events (for instance, a “research” agent could output findings that get queued as an input message to a “writing” agent). [\[insiderllm.com\]](https://www.insiderllm.com/guides/how-openclaw-works/), [\[insiderllm.com\]](https://www.insiderllm.com/guides/how-openclaw-works/) [\[deepwiki.com\]](https://deepwiki.com/openclaw/openclaw) [\[insiderllm.com\]](https://www.insiderllm.com/guides/how-openclaw-works/)
3.  **Context Assembly & Prompting:** When an event is about to be processed, the agent runtime gathers everything needed for context. This includes loading the relevant agent’s configuration (which model to use, any special instructions), reading the agent’s memory files (daily notes, long-term memory) and the last few messages of conversation history from the session log, and then constructing the **full prompt** as described above. The prompt will contain the system sections (tools, skills, etc.) plus the most relevant parts of recent history and memory. This assembly is handled by functions like `buildAgentSystemPrompt`, `resolveBootstrapContextForRun`, and `resolveSkillsPromptForRun` in the code. [\[deepwiki.com\]](https://deepwiki.com/openclaw/openclaw), [\[deepwiki.com\]](https://deepwiki.com/openclaw/openclaw) [\[deepwiki.com\]](https://deepwiki.com/openclaw/openclaw/5-agent-system), [\[deepwiki.com\]](https://deepwiki.com/openclaw/openclaw/5-agent-system)
4.  **Model Invocation:** Next, the agent’s LLM is invoked with the assembled prompt. OpenClaw is **model-agnostic** – by default it uses **Anthropic’s Claude** (particularly Claude 2 or “Claude Code” for coding tasks, due to its 100k+ token context window) because of its long context and tool-use friendly style. However, you can configure other providers like OpenAI’s GPT-4 or local models via **Ollama** (which enables running models like LLaMA or Code LLMs on your hardware). The Gateway will route the request to the appropriate model API or local inference backend based on your config. There’s even a failover system – OpenClaw can juggle multiple model APIs, switching if one fails or using different models for different agents. The model’s **streaming reply** (token-by-token) is captured and parsed in real-time by the agent runtime. [\[github.com\]](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw) [\[aicybr.com\]](https://aicybr.com/blog/openclaw-guide) [\[deepwiki.com\]](https://deepwiki.com/openclaw/openclaw), [\[deepwiki.com\]](https://deepwiki.com/openclaw/openclaw)
5.  **Tool Use & Choreography:** A hallmark of OpenClaw is that the AI can **perform actions**. How this works under the hood is that OpenClaw’s system prompt and the Pi agent core encourage the model to output special tokens or JSON indicating a tool request (for example, the model might produce a chunk of output like `[@"tool": "exec", "args": "ls /home"]`). The agent runtime recognizes this and pauses the model’s stream to execute the requested tool **function** in the Node.js environment. The tool (which could be anything from a web search, to running a code script, to sending a message via an API) runs on your machine with the appropriate permissions, and it returns a result (e.g., the output of a command or the content of a webpage). The agent then incorporates that result into its ongoing reasoning – often by continuing the LLM completion with the new information. This loop of *model proposes an action → action executed by the system → result fed back into model* can repeat multiple times in one session turn, enabling **multi-step workflows** to be carried out autonomously by the agent. For example, if you ask the assistant to “check my calendar and email me any urgent events for tomorrow,” the agent might use a series of tools: first calling a calendar API (via a tool) to get events, then calling an email-sending tool to draft messages. OpenClaw’s orchestration makes this seamless – it’s essentially implementing the ReAct pattern (reasoning interleaved with actions) entirely on your local machine using the LLM’s outputs. [\[deepwiki.com\]](https://deepwiki.com/openclaw/openclaw), [\[insiderllm.com\]](https://www.insiderllm.com/guides/how-openclaw-works/) [\[deepwiki.com\]](https://deepwiki.com/openclaw/openclaw/5-agent-system), [\[deepwiki.com\]](https://deepwiki.com/openclaw/openclaw/5-agent-system)
6.  **Response Delivery & State Updates:** Once the agent has finished processing the event (either returning a final answer or indicating no action was needed), the result is sent back through the Gateway to the originating channel. For a chat message, this means the assistant’s reply is posted in Slack/WhatsApp/etc. For a scheduled task or webhook, the result might be a notification message or simply a logged action. The **session log** is then updated with the latest interaction (including any tool actions taken and the assistant’s final answer) and the cycle is complete. The Gateway then moves on to the next event in the queue, if any. This continuous looping through queued events – combined with the ability of heartbeats, crons, and webhooks to generate new events regularly – is what gives OpenClaw its *autonomous, self-starting behavior*. The “assistant” is essentially always running, checking for triggers and responding with the help of the LLM and tools. [\[deepwiki.com\]](https://deepwiki.com/openclaw/openclaw), [\[deepwiki.com\]](https://deepwiki.com/openclaw/openclaw) [\[insiderllm.com\]](https://www.insiderllm.com/guides/how-openclaw-works/), [\[insiderllm.com\]](https://www.insiderllm.com/guides/how-openclaw-works/)

**Technologies & Frameworks:** OpenClaw is primarily built in **TypeScript/Node.js**, not Python, and it does **not use LangChain** or similar agent frameworks – instead it implements its own orchestration logic (via the Pi agent core library and custom code). Key dependencies include: **Baileys** (WhatsApp Web API) for WhatsApp integration, **grammY** (Telegram bot framework), **Slack Bolt** for Slack bots, and platform SDKs or web APIs for others (Discord, Microsoft Teams via the Bot Framework/Graph API, etc.). The “brain” of the agent is abstracted through the `@mariozechner/pi-*` family of libraries – this is sometimes called the **Pi Agent** or “Claude Code” runtime. It provides a standardized way to interact with LLMs (Anthropic, OpenAI, etc.) and handle complex streaming, function calling, and tool invocation patterns in code. On the front-end, the project includes a web-based **Control UI** (built likely with React or a similar JS framework) served from the local Node.js process. There is also a **macOS app** (written in Swift) and mobile apps that act as “portal” nodes to the same core assistant. These aren’t necessary for core functionality but provide convenient interfaces (e.g. the macOS menu bar app) and native mobile integration. [\[repovive.com\]](https://repovive.com/roadmaps/openclaw/what-is-openclaw/openclaw-vs-ai-agent-frameworks) [\[deepwiki.com\]](https://deepwiki.com/openclaw/openclaw) [\[deepwiki.com\]](https://deepwiki.com/openclaw/openclaw/5-agent-system), [\[deepwiki.com\]](https://deepwiki.com/openclaw/openclaw/5-agent-system)

**Reproducing the Core Functionality (Electron & Custom Workflows):** To build a similar assistant in a more secure, local-first manner, you can mirror OpenClaw’s architecture with a streamlined setup. Essentially, you would create a local **backend service** (which could be a Node.js process, possibly packaged with **Electron** or run separately) that plays the role of the **Gateway** – handling events, maintaining state, and interfacing with an LLM – and an **Electron front-end** that provides a chat UI (this could simply load a web page that communicates with the backend via WebSockets or HTTP, similar to how OpenClaw’s browser-based Control UI works). The communication channels can be as simple or complex as needed: for a purely local assistant, you might skip external messenger integrations and instead just use the Electron app’s chat window as the interface (OpenClaw’s equivalent is the “webchat” channel, denoted by session key `main:main` for a direct local conversation). If you **do** want Microsoft Teams integration, you can follow OpenClaw’s approach by writing a connector that interfaces with the Teams Bot Framework/Graph API – in OpenClaw this is provided as an optional plugin (`@openclaw/msteams`) that you install and configure with an Azure Bot application ID and secret. The **LLM agent** can be accessed via APIs (OpenAI, Anthropic, etc.) or even run locally (there are tools like Ollama or text-generation-webui for serving local models) – OpenClaw demonstrates how to abstract the model provider behind a common interface so that switching from one model to another is just a config change. [\[docs.openclaw.ai\]](https://docs.openclaw.ai/) [\[deepwiki.com\]](https://deepwiki.com/openclaw/openclaw) [\[aicybr.com\]](https://aicybr.com/blog/openclaw-guide)

For implementing **tools and skills**, you can take inspiration from OpenClaw’s plugin system. In an Electron app, you might allow certain whitelisted operations (shell commands, file access, web requests, etc.) and expose them to the AI in a controlled manner. OpenClaw’s source shows how each tool is basically a function that the agent can call through structured output, and you can design your agent prompt to use a similar mechanism (for instance, instruct the model that to use a tool it should emit a JSON blob or a special syntax which your program will detect and execute). The **skill** concept in OpenClaw is essentially a packaged workflow: by writing a `SKILL.md` (with instructions or code) and possibly some scripts, a user can add a new capability. Your application could let users define **custom workflows** (sets of instructions or actions) and save them to files – then load these dynamically and let the AI know it can use them. OpenClaw’s Skills mechanism, for example, loads any skill files in the workspace and even provides a central “ClawHub” repository of community-contributed skills. This has enabled rapid expansion of functionality (tens of thousands of skills are available), though it also introduces security considerations. In your safer redesign, you’d want to carefully sandbox or review any code that the AI might execute (OpenClaw uses config-based allow/deny lists and an optional Docker sandbox to mitigate risk). [\[deepwiki.com\]](https://deepwiki.com/openclaw/openclaw), [\[insiderllm.com\]](https://www.insiderllm.com/guides/how-openclaw-works/) [\[insiderllm.com\]](https://www.insiderllm.com/guides/how-openclaw-works/) [\[deepwiki.com\]](https://deepwiki.com/openclaw/openclaw), [\[deepwiki.com\]](https://deepwiki.com/openclaw/openclaw)

**Conclusion:** In summary, OpenClaw works by combining a **local orchestration backend** (the Gateway) with a flexible AI **agent** that can leverage tools and saved context. All key data – from config to conversation logs and memory – is stored locally in files for transparency and persistence. The assistant’s seemingly autonomous behavior (checking your email, sending messages on its own, performing multi-step tasks) is achieved through a mixture of **event-driven prompts** (timers, crons, webhooks that trigger pre-scripted prompts) and the LLM’s ability to plan actions using tools. OpenClaw’s architecture is quite comprehensive with many moving parts (multi-channel messaging, multiple agents, real-time streaming, etc.), but the *core idea* is that a single, local service mediates between your *commands/chats* and an AI agent that can both converse *and* take actions. Armed with this knowledge, you can design your own “safer” personal assistant: for example, running a minimal Node/Electron-based gateway that connects only to a local chat UI (and/or a Teams bot) and exposing only the specific “skills and workflows” that you build into it. By following OpenClaw’s pattern – **a persistent process with an event queue, a configurable prompt+tools system, and file-based memory storage** – you can recreate its fundamental capabilities in a custom application, harnessing the power of LLMs while retaining full control over the AI’s environment and permissions. [\[insiderllm.com\]](https://www.insiderllm.com/guides/how-openclaw-works/) [\[insiderllm.com\]](https://www.insiderllm.com/guides/how-openclaw-works/), [\[insiderllm.com\]](https://www.insiderllm.com/guides/how-openclaw-works/) [\[insiderllm.com\]](https://www.insiderllm.com/guides/how-openclaw-works/), [\[repovive.com\]](https://repovive.com/roadmaps/openclaw/what-is-openclaw/openclaw-vs-ai-agent-frameworks)
